mnemonics.title: RedBoot Functions
mnemonics.content: R.E.D.B.O.O.T - RedBoot: Initializes hardware, Downloads images via TFTP, Manages Flash (FIS), Boots applications, Offers debugging (GDB), Operates via CLI, Transfers control, Stores config in flash

mnemonics.title: RedBoot Boot Sequence
mnemonics.content: B.O.O.T - Basic Initialization, Operating system loads, Opens drivers (flash/network), Transfers to application

mnemonics.title: eCos HAL Components
mnemonics.content: H.A.L - Hardware Abstraction Layer provides platform-specific code for: Architecture variants, Board-level init, Device drivers

acronyms.usage: Manages bootable images stored in flash memory
Network protocol for downloading/uploading firmware images
Source-level debugger for remote debugging via RedBoot stub
Network protocol for IP configuration in network booting
Open-source OS upon which RedBoot is built
RedBoot's mechanism for flash memory management

visualTips.visualization: Draw a layered diagram: Top layer = Applications, Middle = RedBoot CLI/GDB stub, Bottom = eCos HAL, Base = Hardware (Flash, RAM, Network)
Show flash memory divided into: RedBoot partition (runtime), FIS directory (image table), User data area, Config storage
Create flowchart: Power ON → Hardware Init (HAL) → Load RedBoot → Initialize drivers → Display prompt OR Load & execute image → Transfer to app
Diagram: Embedded Device → BOOTP Request → Server assigns IP → TFTP Request → Server sends image → Device receives & stores → Boot

analogies.analogy: RedBoot is like the BIOS in a PC - it initializes hardware and boots the operating system, but for embedded devices instead of desktops
FIS is like a simple file manager for flash memory, keeping track of where bootable images are stored and their sizes
GDB stub in RedBoot is like a video call for debugging - it lets the developer (GDB on PC) see and control the embedded device remotely
TFTP is like a delivery service for firmware - simple and fast, but without verification (unlike FTP's receipt confirmation)
